title: Influence of twist angle on ultrafast charge separation in WS2-graphene heterostructures
authors: Niklas Hofmann, Leonard Weigl, Johannes Gradl, Stiven Forti, Domenica Convertino, Camilla Coletti, Isabella Gierz
abstract: Van der Waals (vdW) heterostructures, formed by stacking two-dimensional materials, offer highly tunable electronic and optical properties, with the twist angle between layers acting as a critical tuning parameter. While its impact on moiré patterns, band structure, and correlated states is well-established, the influence of twist angle on ultrafast charge transfer remains controversial. Here, we employ time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (trARPES) to directly probe ultrafast charge transfer in epitaxially grown WS\textsubscript{2}-graphene heterostructures with twist angles of 0$^{\circ}$ and 30$^{\circ}$. Upon photoexcitation at $\hbarω= 3.1\,\mathrm{eV}$, we observe efficient charge separation at 0$^{\circ}$, while at 30$^{\circ}$, electron and hole transfer occur at similar rates. Our results highlight the crucial role of the twist angle in controlling charge separation efficiency, offering valuable insights for designing vdW heterostructures for applications in photovoltaics and optoelectronics.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the twist angle is a critical control parameter for charge separation in WS2-graphene heterostructures. Using trARPES, the authors show that while 0-degree alignment enables efficient separation, a 30-degree misalignment leads to simultaneous electron and hole transfer. These findings provide essential insights for engineering next-generation optoelectronic and photovoltaic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates how the twist angle between layers in van der Waals heterostructures influences the efficiency of ultrafast charge separation.
main_result: At a 0 degree twist angle, efficient charge separation is observed, whereas at 30 degrees, electron and hole transfer occur at similar rates, preventing separation. The electron transfer rate itself appears independent of the twist angle, potentially due to tunneling through sulfur vacancies.
method: The researchers used time- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (trARPES) to probe population dynamics and band shifts following photoexcitation.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of epitaxially grown WS2-graphene heterostructures with twist angles of 0 and 30 degrees, where WS2 islands are grown via CVD on hydrogen-intercalated epitaxial graphene on SiC(0001).
key_observables: Population dynamics (gain/loss in graphene Dirac cone and WS2 bands), transient band gap renormalization, and transient band shifts (upward/downward shifts).
important_parameters: Twist angles of 0 and 30 degrees; pump photon energy of 3.1 eV; temporal resolution of 200 fs; energy resolution of 350 meV.
main_assumptions: Fitting of WS2 valence bands assumed fixed energy differences between spin-split bands and fixed spectral weights based on equilibrium data.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows LEED data for twist angle confirmation; Fig 2 displays equilibrium and pump-induced ARPES spectra; Fig 3 shows population dynamics and Dirac cone changes; Fig 4 tracks evolution of band positions and charging shifts; Figs 5-7 detail the methodology for extracting band positions from EDCs and MDCs.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural characterization via LEED, moves to the presentation of trARPES spectral snapshots, analyzes population dynamics and band shifts for both twist angles, compares results to theoretical energy barrier models, and concludes with a discussion on the mechanism of charge transfer.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
